speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll number three.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I wor-- I went to work in London, and I went to work for the film industry. I don't know why, but I had a feeling that I liked it, which I did. And while I was-- my first job was working for a lady, Miss Greenfield-- Miss Stella Greenfield, who was actually a Mrs. Cohen. But funnily enough, her husband had changed his name to Edwards, because Cohen was too Jewish for his business. I just thought I'd tell you that, that that's what they did in England. Jewish people did that when their names were too foreign, or too Jewish. Not necessarily Jewish, but foreign. The English are sort of strange like that. They sort of make fun of-- like we did during the war with these funny names, when we were listening to Alvar Lidell on the BBC, and we heard these funny names from these little towns in North Africa, where Rommel was beaten out of and General Montgomery had taken over the town of whatever-- La La La or Boo Boo Boo, or Puk Puk was another name. And the British are very strange. They like Smith, Jones, Robinson, Turner, Taylor, things like that. So I just mentioned that about this Miss Greenfield. And working with me, in her department, was this other young lady, and we became friendly. She was Jewish, so I sort of clung to her, because I wanted to just keep myself with anything Jewish. I decided, I'm Jewish, I want to be Jewish, and I want to be with Jews. And we became very, very friendly. And she had, and still has, a twin sister who also worked in the film industry, and we used to meet for lunch. And we had other friends in the film industry, and we had, like, a little clique. And we were all Jewish. I was-- I had the time of my life when I worked in London. Then I moved from this particular job to another job, to the [? Rankel ?] organization. And I also worked for a Jewish man. And I still was friendly with my twin girlfriends, and I am to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a secretary. And I had had-- after eight years. I think it was eight years? Six years. I decided-- and I used to run backwards and forwards to Holland. I loved to go to Holland. I loved my family. I-- I really felt very close with them. And I used to go for weekends. It was very cheap in those days, to just run backwards and forwards for a weekend. I'd leave on a-- a Friday, and come back either Monday morning or late Sunday night. And all my holidays, I spent in Holland. And I just loved it. And I-- until the time I decided, I might as well live there. And I did. I wor-- I made application for a work permit. And I didn't need a work permit, because my father, having been born there and still not being a British subject-- he still remained a Dutch citizen. We went to the Dutch consul, who my father knew, and I got a declaration of Dutch citizenship. I personally got-- got it. So I didn't need any work permits or anything like that to get into Holland. And I did. I went-- I went there, I applied to a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] an oil company. I applied to two or three oil companies, because they needed English-speaking people there. I knew that. And I couldn't get a job anywhere. And I couldn't understand why, because I was an excellent secretary, and I came loaded with recommendations, and I was top notch. But I did manage to get a job with Caltex, California Texas Oil Company. And why they took me, I don't know, because my name was Jacoby, which I understand is a very Jewish name. But apparently over there in Holland, it sort of could be one way or the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they took me, and my cousin said to me, don't tell them you're Jewish. And I was very affronted at that. Well, why not? Don't tell them you're Jewish. Don't tell anyone you are Jewish. People don't have to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was still afraid, of course, because he had been through the war. And he had to pretend different things, and he had been abused, and-- anyway. He had a lot of bad things happen to him, and his father also. And he'd lost a lot of cousins, and uncles, and aunts. So he was very scared. He just kept instilling into me, don't tell anybody you are Jewish. But we once went to a Jewish wedding while I was living in Holland, and there I met a very nice young woman who was also a guest. And somehow or other we got very close, and we are, to this day, very, very close friends. She's a survivor. She lives in Israel. When I lived in Holland, I was there for six years. And I was in touch with quite a few Jewish people. But they were either very, very young or very, very old. Most of them were married, and I wanted-- by that time, I decided I wanted to get married. But there weren't enough young men in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I had a girlfriend from London who had come to the United States with her parents, and we still corresponded. And she lived in New York, so she said, why don't you come here? I can get you a job, and you with your qualifications.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, while I was in Holland, I had risen in the ranks at this oil company from being a typist in the purchasing department to being executive assistant to the vice president in charge of something or other. I don't remember what. But I had my own office with my name on the door and I had a telephone and I could call around the world. And it was just wonderful. And I had gotten my own apartment, and furnished it, and I was sitting on top of the world in Holland. However, I was single, and there weren't enough Jews there for me. And I wanted to get married. So I gave it all up and came to United States. And I went to the offices-- the head office of the California Texas Oil Corporation, and I couldn't get a job as dishwasher there. As soon as they saw my name, and I went with piles and piles of recommendations, and how wonderful--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 196-- 1961. January 1961. Because I had-- that's right. I left-- I left Southampton December, 1960, and had the worst crossing of the Atlantic. Took nine days instead of five. And I wanted to be on-- see, I-- I like adventure. So I decided I wanted to be on board an ocean liner during the new year. Worst mistake. Terrible. Never cross the Atlantic in the middle of the winter. All right, it was an experience. I survived. And it was, on reflection, it was very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came to the United States, as I say, and I walked all over Manhattan. And within three weeks, I knew Manhattan upside, downside, cross side, any side. And the subway system became my bible, the-- the maps. And people who-- real New Yorkers said, you know more about New York than we who were born here. I said, well of course. You probably go to London, and know more about London than I'll ever know. When you live in a place. Anyway. So I surrounded myself in New York, which was just wonderful, wonderful. I-- my eyes opened up. It was just marvelous. Everything was so open. And nobody was afraid to say that they were Jewish. They had open cemeteries. They weren't tucked away in some God-forsaken place. They had synagogues all over the place. Oh yes, I have to say that, when I lived in London, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a-- an open space on the street where we lived. and they had-- a group of Jews wanted to build a synagogue there. And they went and they canvassed all the neighbors if you were-- would be in favor, would you mind? Naturally, would we mind? No, of course we wouldn't mind. However it didn't go through, because the majority of people didn't want a synagogue there. Eventually that group of people, what they did, they bought a church that couldn't afford to keep itself running. They bought a church, and they turned it into a synagogue. So the-- the goyim were surrounded with Jew-- by Jews anyway. What can I tell you? We belonged to that synagogue, and I had the greatest fun painting this church and doing it and helping them, and it-- it was wonderful. But I'm digressing. So I'm now back in New York, and I'm just thrilled to pieces to be here. It's just wonderful. All these Jewish restaurants and you-- and everything Jew-- everything that I was searching for was here, was in New York. And nobody was scared. Nobody changed their names. The-- It was just-- just marvelous, and I loved it. I suppose I did meet a little anti-semitism. I suppose, I am not sure. When I did go off to jobs-- not this oil company place, but other jobs-- I suppose I did. But it was very subtle, and really, I didn't care. It didn't bother me. You don't want me because I'm Jewish? There are so many others that I don't care. When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married 1962, March. No, 1963. Sorry. 1963. March 10, 1963. I got married twice. I got married in New York, in the town hall-- city hall. And then we went to London for the religious ceremony because the rules were that we couldn't-- first of all, we were both working. We couldn't spare. We had to spend three full weeks in England before getting permission. The law is that you can't get married until you've spent three full weeks there. So we couldn't afford to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I spoke with the lady at the synagogue and I said, well, how about if we are already married, legally? Well, no problem. So that's what we did. And then the following week, we flew to-- to London, and then we had the proper wedding. We had a nice party in New York for all my husband's friends and my new friends that I had made. And then we had the big party with the gown and the veil, and the whole-- the whole-- What was your life-- what has your life been like since that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I got married? Very nice. Yes. I lived in New York, in a Jewish neighborhood. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we belonged to a synagogue. We weren't strictly, strictly shomer shabbat at that time. But I was strictly Kosher, and I had separate dishes, and I was very particular. And I really enjoyed it. And we had Jewish neighbors. And then when my daughter was born, I decided, she's not going to go through the turmoil that I went through about being told you're Jewish, but that's about it. And knowing that you're different, but that's about it. I'm going to give this child a proper Jewish education. She's going to know she's Jewish, and work at it, and show something. So as soon as she was old enough, she went to Jewish nursery school, and Jewish kindergarten. And she went to Yeshiva all the way through. And then I didn't become strictly, strictly shomer shabbat. But I didn't drive on the shabbat, and I still don't, unless it's an emergency. But as the years went by, my daughter became sort of like me, only more so. Whereas I just wanted to acknowledge that I was Jewish and I wanted my parents to be more Jewish, my daughter became more religious, which is OK with me. And she's-- she's living the life that she wants, and she loves it. And I'm very happy for her and I think it's wonderful. So I finally achieved something with my search for Judaism. I gave birth to a sort of a rebbetzin, I suppose. She's going to be. Her husband is a rabbinical student, among other things. And she's very, very happy, and I'm very happy, and my husband's very happy about that. And that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you Mrs. Feldman. I want to really thank you for participating in this project, the Survivors of the Shoah.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Feldman, please introduce your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Charles-- Charles Feldman. We have been married since March the 10th, 1963, which is 32 years. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he has a nice story to tell you. A few nice-- not so nice, but a few--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are now living in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're living-- oh, I beg your pardon. [LAUGHTER] We're living in Miami Beach, Florida, United States. And we have been here, I think, going on nine years. We came here from New York with our daughter. And we married her off from this house. And she's-- thank goodness, she's also living in Florida, in Hollywood, so she's not too far away. And she's very happily married to a very nice young man by the name of Avi Friar, who is a rabbinical student as well as being youth director of the Young Israel of Hollywood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Feldman, you are retired? Are you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is a photograph of my mother and father, Hilda and Phillip Jacoby. It was taken sometime in the 1960s-- probably '64, '65-- In London. They were at a-- an engagement party and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somewhere in London. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of Lily Jacoby, age three. It was taken at a studio in London. Well, I was about three years old, so in the ear-- early '30s. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This dress was a dress that my grandmother bought me in America, and I just loved it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a photograph of my aunt and uncle, Henrietta, known as Etty, and Yop van Klouten from Holland-- Breda, Holland. The photograph was taken in 1932 in Holland. This is another photograph of my uncle, Yop van Klouten, taken in Holland also, in the early '30s. He was a professional violin player. He was married to my aunt, Etty, and they were the two who did not survive Auschwitz, with their four children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a photograph of Sharon, Charles, and Lily Feldman, taken in New York at Adath Israel Synagogue at a Purim celebration. It was also our wedding anniversary. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 198-- '81. This is a photograph of my daughter, Sharon-- Sharon Feldman, now Friar taken at her wedding, obviously, on September the 6th, 1993. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Lily, Sharon, and Charles Feldman, taken at Sharon's wedding. We're walking her down the aisle at Temple Emanu-El in Miami Beach. Taken on her wedding day, September the 6th, 1992.
